titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, 22 December 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Bliss Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from Sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender (1876 - 1960) to his wife, Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). In this letter, Spender expresses dread at the possibility that the 16th (Irish) Division may soon be joining his own 36th (Ulster Division). He asks Lillian to let Sir Edward Carson (1854-1935) know that while the officers of the 36th Division and those of various Irish brigades have 'got on excellently', the same is not true of the ordinary soldiers, and according to the men there would have been 'real trouble, if they had not been separating so soon'. Spender then argues that many people, including Ulstermen, do not reckon full with the 'Ulster temperament'. Spender then detours at length along the path of Christmas-related sentiment. He then gives the news that a colleague, Captain W. Lindsall, has gotten engaged, and asks Lillian to send the couple a present worth roughly three pounds In a postscript, Spender mentions that the General is fully aware that he was up with the 107th Brigade, but 'has not said a word on the subject'.Sir Wilfrid Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
22 Dec 15 My Pr You really seem to be getting quite excited over things,
but you mustn't count too much on things. Richardson will I hope travel on with this letter so
he may turn up any day. Mayne has left. Again I hear that the 16 Div is coming here after all
or bits of it. I do hope it's not true. You may not have got my family letter with news of
my visit to the 107 Bge, but you can let Sir EC know
that whereas the our officers have got on excellently with the Dublin
& other Irish Regt, the men have not done so equally, & in
fact Douglas who of course was able to get much more into
touch with the men told us that there would have been
real trouble, if they had not been separating so soon. People
who do not know the Ulster temperament - including many
Ulstermen â do not realize how they play with smouldering
flames when they say that things are all right. Is this my Xmas letter? I think so & my little
Pr will find a corner very warm for her when she
takes the wings of thought to it, & tho' I should say it was anything
but a comfortable corner I have no doubt she can fit herself
in it as snugly as she has done this past 2 1/2 years & more so. I shall remember yo her perhaps just a little more than usual,
tho' that won't be easy because.....(naturally because I have so many
other things to think about ?). If no little parcel is on her table to display to the others
as she likes to do so much, she can smile quietly to herself knowing
that she is getting a really big present this time as soon as the
bw can come home & settle it. Funny little animal I believe
she would almost as sooner have a parcel with pink string than
the 5 bits of red paper called Govt bonds which I hope to be able to
put into her paws & which will I hope bring in at least 20
little golden circles to her afterwards. I had a very pleasant but quiet little dinner with the
7th Div last night & found that Lindsell was being married
on the 4th Jany to a Miss Hollam? Daughter of Admiral Hollam
- any relation? - Any how could you send a wedding
present to Capt. W Lindsell RA Langley House Chichester, choosing
something about 3.00. I have served with him such a
lot that I must remember him & if possible I will send
a letter later. You can select the idea from one of our
presents as to what is likely to be useful. Must stop with my
very best Xmas wishes
to the family
whose letters I hope
to be reading on Saturday, & my best
of best love to that curious little animal
who has occupied so
much if not the whole
of her BW. WB Spender PS The Gen knows I was
up with the 107 Bde but
has not said one word on
the subject
